Specimens will be processed using both standard clinical microbiology methods (culture, PCR) and the SwabSeq protocol, which employs barcoded primer sets for multiplexed amplification of conserved regions and resistance markers (e.g., blaNDM, blaKPC) relevant to the researcher's background. A deep learning-based sequence classifier (e.g., a convolutional neural network trained on known pathogen genomes) will be used to analyze the high-throughput sequencing data, distinguishing true pathogens from background or contaminant sequences. Performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, limit of detection) will be calculated against composite reference standards.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, evidence-based framework for applying SwabSeq to non-respiratory clinical microbiology, directly informing its use in rapid outbreak investigation and healthcare-associated infection surveillance. It will generate validated protocols that bridge high-sensitivity molecular detection (akin to high-sensitivity troponin testing) with the practical demands of processing diverse point-of-care and clinical laboratory specimens.
proposals.novelty: While SwabSeq is established for respiratory viruses, its systematic validation and protocol optimization for a broad panel of bacterial and fungal targets in inhibitory clinical matrices is novel. Integrating a deep learning classifier for sequence analysis specifically tailored to these complex sample types moves beyond standard bioinformatics pipelines used in earlier SwabSeq applications.

proposals.title: Field Validation of a High-Throughput SwabSeq Platform for Asymptomatic Surveillance of Respiratory Pathogens in a Community Setting
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the COVID-19 pandemic underscored the critical need for scalable, sensitive, and cost-effective tools for population-level infectious disease surveillance. Existing point-of-care tests, while rapid, are often limited in throughput and multiplexing capability for broad pathogen panels. SwabSeq, a next-generation sequencing-based barcoding method developed for SARS-CoV-2, offers a promising high-throughput solution but requires rigorous field validation for its utility in asymptomatic screening of diverse respiratory pathogens beyond pandemic triage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the SwabSeq platform can be deployed operationally for large-scale, asymptomatic screening with a sensitivity and specificity comparable to individual RT-PCR assays, and that its multiplexed design will enable efficient detection of co-circulating respiratory pathogens (e.g., influenza, RSV, endemic coronaviruses) in a community cohort.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective field trial enrolling 5,000 asymptomatic individuals from a university campus. Self-collected anterior nasal swabs will be pooled (e.g., 10-plex) using unique molecular barcodes per sample as per the SwabSeq protocol. Libraries will be sequenced on an Illumina MiSeq. We will benchmark performance against gold-standard individual RT-PCR for a 10-plex respiratory viral panel. Data analysis will employ a custom bioinformatics pipeline for demultiplexing, alignment (using BWA), and variant calling to determine per-sample positivity, with statistical analysis of sensitivity, specificity, and limit of detection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, real-world evidence for SwabSeq's operational feasibility and diagnostic accuracy in an asymptomatic population. It will generate a framework for implementing high-throughput genomic surveillance in community settings, directly informing public health strategies for outbreak prevention. The study will also yield a rich dataset on the prevalence of asymptomatic carriage for multiple respiratory viruses.
proposals.novelty: While SwabSeq has been used for symptomatic SARS-CoV-2 testing, its application for proactive, multiplexed surveillance of a broad respiratory pathogen panel in a truly asymptomatic general population is novel. This bridges the gap between high-sensitivity lab methods (like Dr. Garner's work on troponin) and the need for scalable, population-level clinical microbiology tools.

proposals.title: A Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of SwabSeq High-Throughput Metagenomic Sequencing Versus RT-qPCR for Syndromic Respiratory Pathogen Surveillance in Clinical Point-of-Care Networks
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic underscored the critical need for scalable, high-sensitivity diagnostic platforms for population-level pathogen surveillance. While RT-qPCR remains the clinical gold standard, its cost and throughput are limiting for large-scale, multiplexed testing. SwabSeq, a scalable, amplicon-based next-generation sequencing (NGS) platform developed pre-2026, offers a highly multiplexed alternative but requires rigorous economic evaluation to justify its adoption in clinical and public health settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for large-scale, syndromic respiratory testing panels, SwabSeq will demonstrate superior cost-effectiveness compared to RT-qPCR when factoring in throughput, multiplexing capability, and the clinical utility of detecting a broader pathogen profile, despite higher per-run sequencing costs.
proposals.method: We will construct a decision-analytic model comparing SwabSeq to RT-qPCR for a panel of 20+ respiratory pathogens. Cost inputs will be derived from institutional data and literature, encompassing reagents, labor, equipment, and bioinformatics. Effectiveness will be measured in quality-adjusted life-years (QALYs) gained, modeled from test sensitivity/specificity (leveraging Dr. Garner's expertise in high-sensitivity assay validation) and downstream clinical outcomes. We will perform deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity analyses using Monte Carlo simulation to identify key cost drivers and model robustness across varying testing volumes and outbreak scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed economic model for SwabSeq in a clinical syndromic surveillance context, offering actionable data for laboratory directors and public health officials. It will bridge the gap between technical NGS validation and practical implementation, directly informing resource allocation for future outbreak preparedness and routine high-volume testing in point-of-care networks.
proposals.novelty: While cost analyses of NGS exist for genomics, applying a rigorous health economic framework to SwabSeq for multiplexed pathogen detection at the scale of clinical point-of-care networks is novel. This integrates Dr. Garner's focus on high-sensitivity clinical testing with scalable surveillance methods, moving beyond pure performance comparison to actionable implementation science.

proposals.title: CRISPR-Cas12a-Mediated Barcoding for Multiplexed, High-Sensitivity Detection of Cardiac and Antimicrobial Resistance Biomarkers via Next-Generation Sequencing
proposals.motivation: Point-of-care (POC) diagnostics for cardiac troponin I (cTnI) and antimicrobial resistance (AMR) genes require both high sensitivity and multiplexing capability to guide rapid clinical decisions. Current POC cTnI assays lack the multiplexing needed for comprehensive panels, while surveillance for healthcare-associated infections (e.g., carbapenemase-producing Pseudomonas) and fungal pathogens requires laborious, separate tests. A method combining the specificity of CRISPR diagnostics with the throughput of sequencing could revolutionize multiplexed pathogen and biomarker detection in clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that coupling the sequence-specific collateral cleavage activity of CRISPR-Cas12a with unique DNA barcodes for each target will enable the simultaneous, ultrasensitive detection of multiple nucleic acid targets (e.g., cTnI mRNA, NDM-1, fungal DNA) in a single reaction, read out via next-generation sequencing (NGS).
proposals.method: We will design specific CRISPR RNA (crRNA) guides for targets including human cTnI, the NDM-1 carbapenemase gene, and fungal ITS regions. Each crRNA will be linked to a unique, inert DNA barcode. Upon target recognition, activated Cas12a will cleave its cognate barcode, releasing it. All barcodes—cleaved and intact—will then be amplified and sequenced on an Illumina MiSeq. The ratio of cleaved to uncleaved barcodes, quantified via a custom bioinformatics pipeline (using tools like Cutadapt and DESeq2 for normalization), will provide digital, quantitative detection of each target with attomolar sensitivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel diagnostic platform that merges CRISPR specificity with NGS multiplexing, enabling a >10-plex panel from a single sample. It will directly extend Dr. Garner's work on high-sensitivity POC cTnI and infection surveillance by providing a unified method for detecting cardiac, bacterial, and fungal targets with clinical lab-level sensitivity in a rapid, care-focused workflow.
proposals.novelty: While CRISPR diagnostics (e.g., SHERLOCK, DETECTR) and NGS barcoding exist separately, using CRISPR's collateral activity to directly gate the sequencing readout of specific barcodes for quantitative, multiplexed detection is a novel integration, moving beyond fluorescence-based readouts to a digital, highly scalable format.

proposals.title: Miniaturized SwabSeq: A Portable, Low-Cost Sequencing Platform for Point-of-Care Detection of Cardiac Troponin and Multi-Pathogen Panels
proposals.motivation: High-sensitivity cardiac troponin I (hs-cTnI) testing is critical for rapid acute myocardial infarction diagnosis but is largely confined to central labs. Simultaneously, rapid, multiplexed pathogen detection, as highlighted by fungal and carbapenemase-producing organism outbreaks, is needed for infection control. Current point-of-care (POC) solutions, like lateral flow or simplified immunoassays, lack the multiplexing capability and analytical sensitivity of next-generation sequencing (NGS). A portable, low-cost NGS platform would enable decentralized, high-sensitivity, multiplexed testing for both cardiac and infectious disease applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the SwabSeq protocol—a scalable, amplicon-based NGS method for SARS-CoV-2 testing—can be successfully miniaturized and adapted to a portable, low-resource device using a small-footprint, low-cost sequencer (e.g., Oxford Nanopore Technologies MinION Mk1C) to achieve clinically relevant sensitivity for hs-cTnI detection and multiplexed pathogen identification at the point of care.
proposals.method: We will adapt the SwabSeq wet-chemistry workflow for a microfluidic cartridge designed for POC use, integrating cell lysis, RNA/DNA extraction, and multiplexed RT-PCR/amplification. The assay will target a panel including the hs-cTnI gene (as a proof-of-concept for direct RNA expression or surrogate viral RNA detection) and common outbreak pathogens (e.g., fungal targets, carbapenemase genes). Amplified products will be sequenced on a MinION Mk1C. A custom, lightweight deep learning model (a 1D convolutional neural network) will be developed for basecalling and rapid sequence classification directly on the device's companion tablet, optimizing for speed and accuracy in low-signal conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-of-its-kind, fully integrated portable NGS device capable of multiplexed, sequence-specific detection at the POC. It will provide validation data for a novel hs-cTnI molecular detection strategy and a rapid outbreak screening tool. The project will establish a framework for deploying high-complexity genomic testing in low-resource and clinical point-of-care settings, bridging a critical gap between central lab sequencing and simple POC diagnostics.
proposals.novelty: While SwabSeq demonstrated high-throughput lab-based testing, its miniaturization for POC use with a focus on cardiac biomarkers is unexplored. Combining a portable sequencer with a tailored deep learning pipeline for rapid, on-device analysis of a combined cardiac-infectious disease panel represents a significant advance over single-analyte POC tests.

proposals.title: A Barcoded, Multiplexed Point-of-Care Platform for Rapid Host Gene Expression Profiling to Discriminate Bacterial from Viral Infections
proposals.motivation: Current syndromic diagnostics for acute infections often fail to rapidly distinguish bacterial from viral etiologies, leading to inappropriate antibiotic use. Pre-2026 research has identified specific host mRNA signatures (e.g., IFI27, LAX1) that accurately classify infection type, but existing profiling methods like RT-qPCR or microarrays are too slow or complex for clinical point-of-care (POC) use. A rapid POC test for host response could transform antimicrobial stewardship in emergency and primary care settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a previously developed barcoded, multiplexed chemiluminescence POC platform, adapted to detect a panel of host mRNA biomarkers, can rapidly and accurately classify the etiology of acute respiratory infections as bacterial or viral based on the host transcriptional signature.
proposals.method: We will adapt our established barcoded chemiluminescence lateral flow assay platform, previously used for high-sensitivity cardiac troponin I detection, to a multiplexed RNA detection format. Specific oligonucleotide capture probes for a pre-defined 3-5 gene host mRNA signature (e.g., from published studies by Sweeney et al. or Tsalik et al.) will be conjugated to distinct barcodes. The assay will integrate isothermal RNA amplification (e.g., RPA or NASBA) with barcode detection. A compact, deep learning-assisted reader (using a lightweight CNN architecture like MobileNetV2) will analyze the multiplexed barcode signal pattern to output an etiology classification.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first proof-of-concept for a rapid, instrumented POC test that classifies infection by profiling host response, not pathogen. It will provide critical validation data on the technical feasibility of translating complex host gene signatures into a simple, rapid format. This directly addresses a major unmet need in antimicrobial stewardship and acute care diagnostics.
proposals.novelty: While host-response signatures are known, their implementation has been confined to central lab RNA-seq or PCR. The novelty lies in repurposing a POC platform designed for protein detection (cTnI) to multiplexed host RNA detection, creating a radically faster, deployable version of this diagnostic paradigm. This 'inverse problem' approach at the POC is unexplored.

proposals.title: A Fully Automated, High-Throughput Platform for Multiplexed Serological Screening Using Deep Learning-Assisted Point-of-Care Assay Technology
proposals.motivation: Population-level serology studies, crucial for understanding immunity to pathogens and vaccine efficacy, are bottlenecked by the manual, low-throughput nature of current point-of-care (POC) assays. Garner's recent work on deep learning-assisted chemiluminescence POC assays for high-sensitivity troponin demonstrates the potential for sensitive, rapid testing, but scaling this for thousands of samples remains a significant logistical and technical challenge. Automating the assembly and reading of these devices is essential to translate sensitive POC diagnostics from individual clinical care to large-scale epidemiological research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating robotic liquid handling for automated device assembly with a convolutional neural network (CNN)-based image analysis pipeline for reading will enable reliable, high-throughput serological testing while maintaining the high sensitivity and specificity of the original POC assay.
proposals.method: We will adapt Garner's established chemiluminescence POC assay format onto a standardized microplate-compatible cartridge. A liquid handling robot (e.g., Hamilton STAR or Opentrons OT-2) will be programmed for automated sample and reagent loading. For reading, we will deploy a high-resolution plate imaging system to capture chemiluminescence signals. A pre-trained CNN architecture (e.g., ResNet-50) will be fine-tuned on a large dataset of assay images to automate signal quantification and classification, moving beyond the single-device analysis of prior work to parallel processing of entire plates.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, automated platform capable of processing hundreds of POC serology assays per day, dramatically increasing throughput for population studies. It will provide a methodological blueprint for scaling other sensitive POC diagnostics (e.g., for cardiac or infectious disease markers) for large-scale screening. The resulting open-source CNN model and robotic protocols will be a significant resource for the diagnostic research community.
proposals.novelty: While automation and deep learning for assay analysis exist separately, their integration specifically to scale a sensitive, chemiluminescence-based POC format for high-throughput serology is novel. This directly bridges the gap between Garner's expertise in sensitive, rapid cardiac testing and the demands of large-scale public health surveillance.

proposals.title: A Deep Learning-Enhanced, High-Sensitivity Chemiluminescence Point-of-Care Platform for Multiplexed Detection of Hundreds of Respiratory Pathogens
proposals.motivation: Current syndromic panels for respiratory infections are limited to ~20-40 targets, missing a vast array of known and emerging pathogens, which hinders precise diagnosis and outbreak surveillance. The COVID-19 pandemic and the pre-2026 rise of antimicrobial resistance (e.g., NDM-producing *P. aeruginosa*) underscore the urgent need for comprehensive, rapid pathogen identification at the point of care to guide therapy and infection control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep learning-assisted chemiluminescence point-of-care (POC) assay, originally developed for ultra-sensitive cardiac troponin detection, can be fundamentally re-engineered to achieve specific, simultaneous detection of hundreds of pathogen nucleic acid targets without sacrificing analytical sensitivity or speed.
proposals.method: We will design a high-density, spatially encoded microarray of pathogen-specific capture probes (bacteria, viruses, fungi) integrated into a single-use POC cartridge. The core innovation is a multi-task convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture, trained on pre-2026 pathogen genome databases, to deconvolve the complex chemiluminescence signal patterns. This CNN will distinguish specific binding from non-specific background and cross-hybridization, enabling accurate multiplexing far beyond traditional analytical limits. The assay workflow will maintain the rapid, sample-in-answer-out format of the original troponin test.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first proof-of-concept for a POC device capable of 'pan-respiratory' pathogen screening, dramatically expanding diagnostic breadth. It will establish a novel computational framework for signal interpretation in ultra-multiplexed, low-volume assays, bridging the gap between high-complexity lab tests and rapid POC needs.
proposals.novelty: While multiplex PCR and microbial cfDNA sequencing exist, applying a deep learning-enhanced, high-sensitivity chemiluminescence POC platform—a paradigm proven for a single protein analyte—to the detection of hundreds of nucleic acid targets is a radical and untested scaling of the technology's core principles.

proposals.title: Dual-pathogen and host-response profiling from a single SwabSeq workflow: A multiplexed, high-sensitivity approach for syndromic infectious disease diagnosis
proposals.motivation: Current syndromic infectious disease panels, including those using SwabSeq, focus on pathogen detection but fail to capture the host's immune status, which is critical for distinguishing colonization from true infection and predicting disease severity. This gap is particularly problematic in clinical settings like sepsis, pneumonia, and healthcare-associated outbreaks (e.g., carbapenemase-producing *Pseudomonas*), where rapid, accurate triage is essential. A method to simultaneously identify pathogens and profile the host transcriptomic response from the same minimally processed sample would provide a more complete diagnostic picture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that host-derived mRNA transcripts, co-captured during SwabSeq-based pathogen detection from nasopharyngeal or blood samples, can be sequenced to generate a specific immune response signature that, when combined with pathogen identification, improves the diagnostic accuracy and clinical stratification of acute infections compared to pathogen detection alone.
proposals.method: We will adapt the established SwabSeq barcoding and multiplexed amplicon sequencing platform. From the same lysate used for pathogen-specific PCR (e.g., for bacteria, fungi, viruses), we will perform targeted reverse transcription and PCR for a panel of ~50 host immune response genes (e.g., IFNG, IL6, TNF, CXCL10, OAS1) using the same sample barcodes. Sequencing data will be demultiplexed, with reads assigned to either the pathogen or host panel. Host gene expression signatures will be analyzed using unsupervised clustering (e.g., UMAP, hierarchical clustering) and supervised machine learning models (e.g., Random Forest, LASSO regression) to identify signatures correlating with specific pathogen classes, infection severity, or bacterial vs. viral etiology.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for a fully integrated, dual-purpose diagnostic assay that provides concurrent pathogen detection and host-response profiling from a single, rapid workflow. It will generate novel data on paired pathogen-host transcriptomic states in clinical samples, moving beyond the binary detection paradigm. The findings could directly inform the development of next-generation point-of-care tests that offer not only 'what is there' but also 'what it is doing to the patient,' aligning with the PI's expertise in high-sensitivity, clinically actionable testing.
proposals.novelty: While SwabSeq is established for highly multiplexed pathogen detection, its repurposing to simultaneously sequence host transcripts from the same barcoded sample without additional processing is novel. This approach uniquely leverages the existing infrastructure to extract complementary host-response data, creating a truly dual-purpose diagnostic tool from a single assay, which has not been previously demonstrated.

proposals.title: A smartphone-based point-of-care platform for rapid antimicrobial susceptibility testing of Gram-positive bacteria and Candida species
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in Gram-positive pathogens (e.g., methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus, MRSA) and fungal pathogens (e.g., Candida auris) is a critical global health threat. Rapid, point-of-care (POC) diagnostics for these organisms are lacking, leading to delays in appropriate therapy and poor outcomes. Extending a validated POC platform to these pathogens would address a significant clinical need for timely, targeted antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: A smartphone-based optical reader, previously validated for Gram-negative bacteria, can be adapted to accurately perform phenotypic antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) for key Gram-positive bacteria and fungal pathogens by quantifying pathogen-specific growth inhibition in the presence of antibiotics or antifungals.
proposals.method: We will adapt a smartphone-based chemiluminescence reader platform, leveraging prior work on deep learning-assisted POC assays. For Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus, Enterococcus spp.), we will optimize lytic agents for ATP release and pair them with a luciferase-based chemiluminescence assay. For Candida species, we will use the fungal-specific metabolite 1,3-β-D-glucan as a detection target. A convolutional neural network (CNN), trained on time-series chemiluminescence image data from spiked clinical samples, will classify samples as susceptible or resistant by detecting growth patterns in the presence of drug panels.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a single, unified POC platform capable of rapid AST for a broad spectrum of bacterial and fungal pathogens, directly at the patient's bedside. It will provide clinical validation data for fungal AST, a major gap in POC diagnostics, and generate a novel image dataset for machine learning models in fungal phenotyping.
proposals.novelty: While smartphone-based AST for Gram-negatives exists, the simultaneous adaptation of a single platform to both Gram-positive bacteria and fungi using distinct, optimized biochemical assays and a unified deep learning analysis pipeline is novel. This moves beyond bacterial diagnostics to integrate the pressing challenge of fungal AMR into a portable clinical tool.

proposals.title: A Deep Learning-Assisted, Rapid Point-of-Care Platform for Multiplexed Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing Directly from Clinical Specimens
proposals.motivation: Rapid antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) directly from patient specimens is critical for combating antimicrobial resistance and improving sepsis outcomes, but current methods are slow or require prior culture. The recent outbreak of NDM-producing Pseudomonas aeruginosa highlights the urgent need for point-of-care (POC) tools to detect resistance mechanisms swiftly. Direct specimen testing could dramatically shorten the time-to-effective-therapy, a key determinant of survival in bloodstream and urinary tract infections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multiplexed, chemiluminescence-based POC assay platform, integrated with a deep learning image analysis pipeline, can accurately perform phenotypic AST directly from urine and blood specimens, providing reliable minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) results within 2 hours.
proposals.method: We will adapt our established deep learning-assisted chemiluminescence POC platform, previously validated for high-sensitivity troponin, for multiplexed bacterial detection and AST. The assay will utilize a panel of lyophilized antibiotics in microfluidic chambers, with bacterial growth inhibition measured via chemiluminescent signals from bacterial metabolism. A convolutional neural network (CNN), specifically a ResNet-50 architecture pre-trained on microbial culture images, will be fine-tuned to analyze the multiplexed chemiluminescence patterns from raw specimens, correlating signal dynamics with MICs and correcting for background interference from host cells and debris.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-in-class POC platform for direct phenotypic AST, potentially reducing the diagnostic timeline from 24-48 hours to under 2 hours. It will provide a novel clinical decision support tool for empiric antibiotic de-escalation or escalation, directly addressing the challenge of healthcare-associated infections. The integration of deep learning for signal interpretation from complex matrices represents a significant advance in POC diagnostic robustness.
proposals.novelty: While POC platforms exist for pathogen detection, applying a deep learning-assisted chemiluminescence assay for multiplexed, quantitative phenotypic AST directly from uncultured clinical specimens is a novel integration. This approach bypasses the culture step entirely, a key distinction from existing rapid AST methods that still require isolated colonies.

proposals.title: A smartphone-integrated LAMP-CRISPR assay for rapid, point-of-care genotypic antimicrobial susceptibility testing from positive blood cultures
proposals.motivation: The rise of multidrug-resistant organisms, such as NDM-producing Pseudomonas aeruginosa, necessitates faster antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) to guide timely, effective therapy. Current phenotypic AST methods require 24-48 hours post-culture positivity, delaying optimal treatment. A rapid, point-of-care molecular AST platform could dramatically shorten this timeline, directly impacting patient care and antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combining loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) with CRISPR-Cas12a detection in a smartphone-read, portable device will enable accurate, culture-independent identification of key carbapenemase resistance genes (e.g., blaNDM, blaKPC) directly from positive blood culture broth within 60 minutes.
proposals.method: We will develop a microfluidic cartridge performing LAMP amplification of resistance gene targets, followed by Cas12a-mediated cleavage of a fluorescent reporter upon target recognition. A custom 3D-printed smartphone adapter will house excitation LEDs and optical filters. The phone camera will capture fluorescence changes, analyzed by a dedicated mobile app using a pre-trained convolutional neural network (CNN) to classify positive/negative results, building on our prior deep learning-assisted point-of-care assay expertise. Validation will use characterized clinical isolates, including carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales and P. aeruginosa.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully integrated prototype for rapid, genotypic AST at the point of care, bridging the gap between molecular diagnostics and clinical decision-making. It will provide a framework for expanding the assay panel to other resistance markers and demonstrate the clinical utility of phone-based diagnostics in infectious disease management.
proposals.novelty: While LAMP and CRISPR have been used separately for detection, their integration into a single, phone-based, sample-to-answer system for direct AST from complex clinical samples like positive blood cultures is novel. The approach moves beyond simple pathogen detection to functional resistance profiling, a critical unmet need in rapid diagnostics.

proposals.title: A Deep Learning-Enhanced Point-of-Care Platform to Discriminate Post-Infectious Autoimmune Sequelae from Persistent Bacterial Infection Using High-Sensitivity Cardiac Biomarkers and Microbial Cell-Free DNA
proposals.motivation: Patients often experience persistent symptoms (e.g., fatigue, myalgia) following treatment for bacterial infections like Pseudomonas aeruginosa, leading to prolonged antibiotic use and potential harm. Pre-2026, the diagnostic paradigm defaults to searching for persistent infection, but emerging evidence suggests post-infectious autoimmune or inflammatory mechanisms may be responsible. This creates a critical need for point-of-care (POC) tools that can rapidly differentiate these etiologies to guide appropriate therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-modal biomarker signature combining high-sensitivity cardiac troponin I (hs-cTnI) as a marker of systemic inflammation or subclinical cardiac strain, with microbial cell-free DNA (mcfDNA) for pathogen detection, can accurately distinguish post-infectious autoimmune-driven symptoms from active, persistent bacterial infection when analyzed by a clinical deep learning model.
proposals.method: We will develop a rapid, chemiluminescence-based POC assay capable of simultaneously quantifying hs-cTnI and specific bacterial mcfDNA (e.g., from P. aeruginosa) from a single blood sample. A convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture, trained on clinical data from patients with resolved infections (symptomatic vs. asymptomatic) and those with culture-confirmed persistent bacteremia, will integrate the quantitative biomarker levels, patient demographics, and core clinical parameters. The model will output a probability score for autoimmune/inflammatory vs. infectious etiology.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, rapid POC diagnostic framework that moves beyond infection-centric testing, directly addressing a major clinical dilemma in antimicrobial stewardship. It will provide preliminary evidence for hs-cTnI as a novel inflammatory biomarker in non-cardiac post-infectious syndromes and demonstrate the utility of integrating mcfDNA with deep learning for etiological discrimination at the bedside.
proposals.novelty: While hs-cTnI is established for cardiac care and mcfDNA for infection detection, their combined use in a POC deep learning model to identify post-infectious autoimmune states is unprecedented. This approach repurposes high-sensitivity cardiac assay technology for a novel inflammatory indication, creating a new diagnostic pathway.

proposals.title: Development and Preclinical Evaluation of a Nucleoside-Modified mRNA-LNP Vaccine Expressing Outer Surface Protein A (OspA) for the Prevention of Lyme Disease
proposals.motivation: Lyme disease, caused by Borrelia burgdorferi, is the most common vector-borne illness in the Northern Hemisphere, with rising incidence and significant long-term morbidity. No human vaccine has been available since LYMErix was withdrawn in 2002, creating a major unmet public health need. The proven success and rapid adaptability of nucleoside-modified mRNA-LNP platforms, as demonstrated by COVID-19 vaccines, offer a timely opportunity to develop a safer, more immunogenic, and easily manufacturable Lyme vaccine.
proposals.hypothesis: A nucleoside-modified mRNA-LNP vaccine encoding Borrelia burgdorferi Outer Surface Protein A (OspA) will elicit high-titer, durable, and functional antibody responses in a murine model, providing protection against challenge with infected ticks.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a codon-optimized mRNA encoding the OspA serotype 1 (ST1) lipoprotein, incorporating 1-methylpseudouridine and formulated in lipid nanoparticles (LNPs). Immunogenicity will be assessed in C3H/HeN mice via a prime-boost regimen, with serum analyzed by ELISA for anti-OspA IgG titers and in vitro borreliacidal assays. Protection will be evaluated by challenging vaccinated mice with B. burgdorferi-infected Ixodes scapularis nymphs, followed by culture and qPCR of tissues to determine bacterial burden. A deep learning-assisted chemiluminescence immunoassay (inspired by Garner's prior work on high-sensitivity troponin) will be adapted to create a rapid, quantitative point-of-care test for anti-OspA antibodies to correlate with protection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide proof-of-concept for a novel mRNA-based Lyme disease vaccine platform, generating critical preclinical immunogenicity and efficacy data. It will also establish a framework for applying advanced, rapid serological assays to vaccine development, bridging Garner's expertise in high-sensitivity point-of-care testing with infectious disease prevention.
proposals.novelty: While OspA is a known vaccine antigen, this proposal applies the cutting-edge, clinically validated mRNA-LNP platform—successful for SARS-CoV-2 but not yet explored for Lyme disease—to overcome historical limitations of protein-subunit vaccines. The integration of a deep learning-assisted serological readout for vaccine immunogenicity represents a novel translational methodology.

proposals.title: The Impact of Diagnostic Turnaround Time on Antimicrobial Stewardship Efficacy: A Randomized Controlled Trial Comparing Rapid Point-of-Care vs. Central Lab Testing in Suspected Sepsis
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship programs (ASPs) are critical for combating resistance, but their effectiveness is often evaluated assuming uniform data availability. In sepsis, delays in pathogen identification and susceptibility testing can force empiric broad-spectrum therapy, potentially reducing ASP impact. The pre-2026 proliferation of rapid, point-of-care (POC) diagnostics for infectious diseases, including molecular and cell-free DNA tests, offers a chance to directly test this assumption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the efficacy of a prospective audit-and-feedback ASP intervention is significantly greater when supported by rapid, POC diagnostic results compared to standard-of-care central laboratory testing, leading to faster appropriate antibiotic de-escalation and shorter durations of therapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, single-center RCT in the emergency department and ICU. Adult patients initiated on broad-spectrum antibiotics for suspected sepsis will be randomized to two arms: (1) a POC testing arm using a multiplex PCR panel (e.g., BioFire® FilmArray® Blood Culture Identification) and plasma microbial cell-free DNA sequencing (e.g., Karius® test) with results in <8 hours, and (2) a standard arm using conventional culture and central lab PCR (24-72 hour turnaround). Both arms will receive identical, protocol-driven daily ASP review. The primary outcome is time to first appropriate antibiotic de-escalation. We will use Cox proportional hazards regression for analysis, adjusting for severity scores.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence quantifying how diagnostic speed modulates ASP effectiveness. It will offer actionable data for healthcare systems deciding on investments in rapid diagnostics and inform the design of next-generation, time-sensitive stewardship protocols.
proposals.novelty: While prior research shows rapid diagnostics improve outcomes, it has not isolated and measured their specific interaction with ASP workflow efficacy, challenging the implicit assumption that stewardship review is equally effective regardless of data latency.

proposals.title: Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of a Deep Learning-Assisted High-Sensitivity Cardiac Troponin Point-of-Care Testing Platform in the Emergency Department
proposals.motivation: High-sensitivity cardiac troponin (hs-cTnI) assays are critical for rapid diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction, but central laboratory testing can cause treatment delays. While novel point-of-care (POC) platforms with deep learning (DL) assistance promise faster, sensitive results, their economic impact on healthcare systems remains unquantified. A formal cost-effectiveness analysis is needed to inform clinical adoption and reimbursement decisions, especially in resource-constrained settings like emergency departments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a DL-assisted, rapid hs-cTnI POC testing system will be cost-effective compared to standard central laboratory testing, primarily by reducing length of stay and improving early clinical decision-making, leading to net savings per patient despite higher per-test costs.
proposals.method: We will construct a decision-analytic Markov model comparing the rapid POC system to standard care. Model parameters (test performance, turnaround times, resource use) will be derived from a prospective observational study in an ED, utilizing the researcher's prior DL-assisted chemiluminescence assay. Costs will be captured via time-driven activity-based costing. We will perform probabilistic sensitivity analysis using Monte Carlo simulation (10,000 iterations) to account for parameter uncertainty and calculate incremental cost-effectiveness ratios (ICERs) against a willingness-to-pay threshold of $100,000 per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive economic evaluation of an AI-enhanced POC troponin test, offering evidence for hospital administrators and policymakers. It will establish a modeling framework for evaluating the cost-effectiveness of other rapid diagnostic technologies in acute care settings, bridging the gap between technical performance and health economic value.
proposals.novelty: While cost-effectiveness analyses of POC tests exist, this work uniquely integrates the economic impact of a DL-optimized assay, modeling the value of algorithmic improvements in sensitivity and rapidity on downstream clinical pathways and costs, beyond simple test accuracy comparisons.

proposals.title: Clinical Impact of a Rapid, Multiplexed Molecular Point-of-Care Test for Bloodstream Infection Diagnosis and Antimicrobial Resistance Prediction in Pediatric Oncology and Stem Cell Transplant Patients
proposals.motivation: Pediatric oncology and hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) patients are at extreme risk for life-threatening bloodstream infections (BSIs) with multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs), as evidenced by outbreaks like NDM-producing Pseudomonas. Delays in pathogen identification (ID) and antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) in these immunocompromised hosts lead to inappropriate empiric therapy, increased mortality, and prolonged hospitalization. Current culture-based methods are too slow, and the clinical utility of novel rapid diagnostics in this specific, vulnerable population is poorly characterized.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementation of a rapid molecular POC diagnostic for ID and resistance marker detection from positive blood cultures will significantly reduce time to optimal antimicrobial therapy and improve clinical outcomes (e.g., 30-day mortality, length of stay) in pediatric oncology/HSCT patients with BSIs compared to standard-of-care microbiology.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, single-center, randomized controlled trial. Pediatric oncology/HSCT patients with a positive blood culture bottle will be randomized 1:1 to an intervention arm (testing with the BioFire® FilmArray® Blood Culture Identification 2 panel or equivalent multiplex PCR at the point-of-care) or a standard-of-care arm (conventional culture and MALDI-TOF ID with phenotypic AST). The primary outcome is time to optimal antimicrobial therapy. Secondary outcomes include time to effective therapy, mortality, and hospital costs. Data will be analyzed using intention-to-treat with Kaplan-Meier survival analysis and Cox proportional hazards models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence (RCT data) on the effectiveness of rapid POC ID/AST in a defined pediatric immunocompromised cohort. It will quantify the impact on time-critical therapeutic decisions and hard clinical endpoints, informing clinical guidelines and cost-benefit analyses for implementing these technologies in pediatric cancer centers.
proposals.novelty: While rapid diagnostics have been studied in general sepsis populations, this proposal uniquely focuses on the highest-risk pediatric subgroup where diagnostic delays are most consequential, and integrates POC testing directly into the pediatric oncology clinical workflow, moving beyond adult-centric or general ICU evaluations.

proposals.title: Clinical Validation of a Rapid Syndromic PCR Panel for Pathogen Identification and Resistance Gene Detection Directly from Positive Blood Cultures
proposals.motivation: Bloodstream infections are life-threatening, and time to appropriate antimicrobial therapy is a critical determinant of survival. Conventional culture-based identification and antimicrobial susceptibility testing from positive blood cultures can take 24-72 hours, leading to prolonged empirical broad-spectrum therapy. Syndromic PCR panels for respiratory and gastrointestinal infections have demonstrated clinical utility by rapidly narrowing pathogen targets, but their application directly to the positive blood culture medium—a complex matrix—remains underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: A commercially available, broad syndromic PCR panel (e.g., BioFire FilmArray Blood Culture Identification panel or similar) can be adapted and validated for use directly from flagged positive blood culture broth, providing accurate pathogen identification and key resistance gene detection (e.g., mecA, vanA/B, blaKPC, blaNDM) with a turnaround time of less than 2 hours from culture positivity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective diagnostic accuracy study using residual positive blood culture broth from hospitalized patients. The index test will be the direct application of a syndromic multiplex PCR panel (specifically the BioFire FilmArray BCID2 panel, which was commercially available pre-2026 and targets >25 pathogens and 10 resistance genes) following a standardized broth processing protocol. The reference standard will be conventional culture, MALDI-TOF identification, and phenotypic susceptibility testing. Statistical analysis will calculate sensitivity, specificity, and positive/negative predictive values for each target. We will also model the potential impact on time-to-effective therapy using a care pathway analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide robust clinical validation data for the use of rapid syndromic PCR directly from positive blood cultures in a real-world setting. It will quantify the test's accuracy and its potential to accelerate the de-escalation of inappropriate antibiotics or the escalation to targeted therapy for resistant organisms, directly addressing antimicrobial stewardship goals. The findings will offer a clear framework for integrating this technology into clinical care pathways for sepsis.
proposals.novelty: While syndromic PCR panels are established for other sample types, this proposal focuses on rigorously validating and optimizing their workflow integration specifically for the critical juncture of a positive blood culture, bridging a gap between rapid molecular diagnostics and conventional microbiology practice. It leverages Dr. Garner's expertise in point-of-care assay validation and clinical implementation, as evidenced in prior work on troponin testing, to a new domain of infectious disease diagnostics.

proposals.title: A National Point-of-Care Network for Real-Time Surveillance of Gram-Negative Carbapenem Resistance: A Feasibility and Implementation Study
proposals.motivation: The rapid spread of carbapenem-resistant Gram-negative bacteria (CR-GNB), such as NDM-producing Pseudomonas aeruginosa, poses a critical public health threat, leading to prolonged outbreaks and high mortality. Current surveillance relies on centralized laboratory testing, causing significant delays in detecting and responding to emerging resistance patterns. A real-time, geographically distributed surveillance system is urgently needed to inform national infection control policies and antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deploying a standardized, rapid point-of-care diagnostic platform across a national hospital network will enable real-time, high-fidelity surveillance of CR-GNB, significantly reducing the time to detect regional outbreaks and resistance trends compared to conventional laboratory-based reporting.
proposals.method: We will establish a pilot network of 20-30 hospitals equipped with standardized, FDA-cleared rapid molecular diagnostic devices (e.g., BioFire FilmArray, Cepheid GeneXpert) for on-site detection of carbapenemase genes (e.g., blaNDM, blaKPC, blaOXA-48). A cloud-based data aggregation platform will be developed using a secure, HIPAA-compliant architecture (e.g., AWS/Azure) to receive de-identified test results in near real-time. Data will be analyzed using spatiotemporal statistical models (e.g., scan statistics) and visualized on an interactive dashboard to identify clusters and trends.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first proof-of-concept for a scalable, real-time national surveillance network for antimicrobial resistance using point-of-care diagnostics. It will generate a novel, high-resolution dataset on CR-GNB epidemiology and establish a framework for rapid public health intervention. The study will also outline the practical challenges and solutions for integrating diverse point-of-care data streams into a unified surveillance system.
proposals.novelty: While point-of-care testing is used for individual patient care, its application as a distributed sensor network for national, real-time bacterial resistance surveillance is novel. This approach moves beyond retrospective, lab-centric reporting to a proactive, geographically granular model enabled by modern data infrastructure.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework to Optimize Empirical Antimicrobial Therapy Using Gram Stain Morphology and Clinical Predictors in the Context of Slow Phenotypic AST
proposals.motivation: Phenotypic antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) often requires 48-72 hours, creating a critical stewardship gap where empirical therapy decisions are made with limited data. Inappropriate initial therapy is linked to increased mortality, morbidity, and antimicrobial resistance. This problem is acute in bloodstream and other serious infections where rapid diagnostics like PCR or MALDI-TOF may not be available or conclusive for resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: A predictive model integrating Gram stain morphology (cocci, rods, yeast) with readily available clinical and epidemiological data (e.g., infection source, local antibiograms, patient comorbidities, prior culture history) can generate optimized, probabilistic empirical therapy recommendations that outperform standard guideline-based approaches in achieving appropriate initial coverage while minimizing unnecessary broad-spectrum use.
proposals.method: We will develop a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from patients with positive blood cultures (2015-2024). Features will include Gram stain result, infection source, demographics, comorbidities, healthcare exposure, and local unit-specific antibiograms. The outcome is the susceptibility profile of the eventual isolate. We will train and compare multiple machine learning classifiers, including gradient boosting machines (XGBoost) and regularized logistic regression, to predict probabilities of resistance to key antibiotic classes. The model output will be used to formulate an algorithmic stewardship protocol that selects the narrowest predicted-effective regimen.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a data-driven, generalizable protocol for enhancing empirical therapy decisions during the AST waiting period. It will quantify the potential improvement in 'time to appropriate therapy' and reduction in unnecessary broad-spectrum exposure achievable by systematically leveraging Gram stain and clinical context. The resulting framework can be integrated into clinical decision support systems.
proposals.novelty: While clinical prediction rules for specific pathogens exist, this approach frames the entire empirical therapy optimization problem as an inverse machine learning task, generating a dynamic, morphology-specific protocol rather than a static rule. It uniquely focuses on maximizing utility from the oldest and most universal microbiological test—the Gram stain—in the modern data science context.

ideas.title: Context-Driven Minimal Biomarker Signature Discovery for Streamlined Diagnostics
ideas.description: This research challenges the one-size-fits-all approach to multiplexed panels by creating a method to derive context-specific minimal assays. It employs a foundational AI model, trained on historical multiplexed data (e.g., from mass cytometry or Luminex panels), to perform in-silico ablation studies and identify the smallest set of biomarkers that retain diagnostic or prognostic power for a defined clinical scenario. The output is not a single universal antigen, but a validated, simplified assay protocol (targeting a very small, optimized panel) tailored to a specific disease stage, subtype, or patient cohort, thereby balancing simplicity with biological necessity.

ideas.title: Causally-Grounded, Concept-Based Antigen Discovery via Inherently Interpretable Diagnostic Models
ideas.description: This research develops a diagnostic algorithm using inherently interpretable AI architectures, such as concept bottleneck models, which are explicitly trained to detect and quantify predefined antigenic concepts from input data. The system's reliance on specific antigen signals is then rigorously tested through a framework of in silico experiments, including systematic counterfactual simulations and concept ablation studies, to distinguish correlation from causation. Finally, the computational findings on critical antigenic concepts are validated against independent experimental data (e.g., ELISA, neutralization assays) to ensure the explanations reflect biologically meaningful drivers of the diagnostic outcome, not just model artifacts.

ideas.title: Edge-AI-CRISPR: A smartphone-interfaced, ultra-portable device for real-time, multiplexed pathogen detection using optimized CRISPR models
ideas.description: This research fuses CRISPR-based nucleic acid detection with edge-optimized machine learning to create a novel, ultra-portable diagnostic device. The core innovation is a tiny, quantized neural network deployed on a low-cost microcontroller within the device, which analyzes the real-time fluorescence or colorimetric signals from a multiplexed CRISPR-Cas assay. This allows the system to automatically distinguish specific pathogen DNA (like Borrelia burgdorferi) from background noise, quantify target concentration, and even identify co-infections from a single reaction, all isothermally. The result is a handheld, battery-powered unit that provides robust, interpretable diagnostic results directly in the field, moving beyond simple 'line present/absent' readouts to sophisticated analysis at the point of need.

ideas.title: Disposable, Self-Developing Immunoassay Card with Direct Visual Scoring
ideas.description: This research develops a credit-card-sized, fully integrated disposable cartridge that performs a complete assay upon sample introduction. The cartridge's microfluidic and reagent-storage design is engineered to not only run the test but also to physically generate a stable, developed visual output—such as a pattern of colored lines, a changing symbol, or a migrating dye front—on its external surface. This output is designed with intrinsic calibration, like a graduated scale or a reference pattern, allowing the user to directly read and interpret the result by visual comparison without a phone, scanner, or reader. The synergy yields a diagnostic tool with the ultimate simplicity of a lateral flow test but with the quantitative capability and integrated fluidic control typically requiring external instrumentation.

ideas.title: Smartphone-Enabled, Field-Deployable CRISPR Colorimetric Assay for Point-of-Care Lyme Disease Detection
ideas.description: This research fuses CRISPR-Cas12a-based isothermal nucleic acid detection for Borrelia burgdorferi with advanced smartphone colorimetric analysis to create a field-deployable diagnostic system. The Cas12a's collateral cleavage activity upon target recognition is linked to a colorimetric reaction, producing a visible color change. A dedicated smartphone app, utilizing ambient light correction algorithms adapted from food safety testing, quantitatively analyzes the color intensity from a paper-based test strip or tube, correcting for variable lighting conditions. This integrated system allows for highly specific, instrument-free, and quantitatively robust detection of Lyme disease DNA directly from a patient sample at the point of care, such as a clinic or pharmacy, enabling rapid clinical decision-making.

ideas.title: Adapt CRISPR-Cas detection with a synthetic biology 'amplification cascade' for ultrasensitive Borrelia detection
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from synthetic biology—the use of designed genetic amplification cascades for sensitive molecular detection—to enhance CRISPR-based diagnostics for Borrelia burgdorferi. In the analogous field, researchers build circuits where a specific molecular input triggers sequential, non-linear amplification steps to produce a robust output from a tiny initial signal. Adapted here, the detection of Borrelia DNA via an initial CRISPR-Cas recognition event would be designed to activate a second, pre-amplified CRISPR-Cas reporter system. This creates a synthetic 'amplification cascade' within the isothermal reaction, significantly boosting sensitivity for low-copy nucleic acids in early Lyme disease, while maintaining the specificity, speed, and field-deployability of CRISPR diagnostics.

ideas.title: Adapting DNA Microarray Automation for High-Throughput Paper-to-Plate Assay Conversion
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from genomics, specifically the solution developed to scale gene expression analysis from blots to DNA microarrays. The analogous solution involved robotic arrayers for miniaturized, parallel probe deposition, automated hybridization stations, and dedicated scanners. Adapted to the research context, this means developing a method where the critical multiplexed capture components of the paper assay are robotically printed in a microarray pattern onto the base of each well in a microplate. This creates a standardized, high-density 'assay plate.' The process is then fully integrated with automated liquid handling for sample and reagent addition, incubation, and washing, followed by high-resolution plate reading for signal detection. This adaptation transforms the low-throughput, manual paper assay into a high-throughput, automated plate-based screening platform suitable for a centralized laboratory environment, mirroring the scalability leap achieved in genomics.

ideas.title: Apply forensic accounting audit techniques to interpret diagnostic AI decisions
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from the field of Financial Forensics and Fraud Detection to the problem of interpreting diagnostic AI decisions. In finance, forensic accountants solve the problem of identifying the root causes of complex outcomes (like fraud) by meticulously tracing transactions and auditing ledgers to pinpoint the most influential, often obfuscated, signals. The analogous solution involves systematic auditing, anomaly isolation, and evidence trail creation. Adapted to the research context, we treat the AI's processing of antigen signals as a financial ledger. The adapted idea is to apply forensic accounting audit techniques—specifically transaction tracing and anomaly detection—to the model's internal 'ledger' of activations. This will allow researchers to systematically trace, isolate, and rank the specific antigen signal pathways the diagnostic algorithm relies on most, creating an auditable, evidence-based explanation that distinguishes critical diagnostic signals from noise, much like a forensic report details the key transactions in a fraud case.

ideas.title: Environmentally-Compensated Reader with Real-Time Image Validation for Paper-Based Immunoassays
ideas.description: This system is a low-cost, portable reader that actively manages the image capture environment to ensure analytical robustness. It features a sealed hood with programmable multi-wavelength LEDs to eliminate ambient light interference and optimize contrast for specific assay chemistries. An integrated microcontroller runs real-time quality checks on focus, alignment, and illumination uniformity, providing immediate user feedback via simple prompts. Only images passing these validation criteria are passed to the analysis algorithm, guaranteeing that automated quantification is performed on consistently high-quality input data, regardless of the external setting.

ideas.title: Incorporate lateral flow assay multiplexing strategies

ideas.title: Ultra-low-waste, reader-reusable diagnostic cartridge with disposable fluidic core
ideas.description: This redesigned point-of-care system uses a two-part architecture to minimize waste and cost. A credit-card-sized, entirely plastic/paper disposable cartridge contains the sample inlet, microfluidic pathways, and dried reagents, but no electronics or complex optics. This 'fluidic core' is inserted into a durable, handheld, reusable reader unit containing the optical sensors, processors, battery, and connectivity. The reader powers the assay, analyzes the signal, and transmits results via a smartphone app. This approach drastically reduces per-test cost and environmental impact while maintaining the simplicity and integration of the original single-use concept.

ideas.title: Integrated on-device whole blood filtration and stabilization system for quantitative point-of-care assays
ideas.description: This redesigned approach incorporates an initial, passive microfluidic filtration and agglutination chamber within the lateral flow device to physically remove red and white blood cells from the sample prior to analysis. The clarified filtrate is then metered into a downstream detection zone containing assay chemistry optimized for the purified matrix, eliminating variable cellular interference. This integrated separation step ensures consistent sample delivery and reagent performance, enabling direct whole blood application while maintaining the precision required for quantitative measurements. The system is designed for single-step user operation, preserving the simplicity of standard lateral flow tests.

ideas.title: Distill deep-learned diagnostic insights into a field-deployable rule-based score
ideas.description: This approach first trains a high-capacity deep learning model on the full multiplex dataset to achieve state-of-the-art diagnostic performance, explicitly capturing complex biomarker interactions. It then applies advanced model distillation and symbolic regression techniques to extract the core logical rules and non-linear relationships that drive the model's predictions. The output is a simple, deterministic diagnostic score or decision tree that explicitly encodes these discovered patterns, ensuring it retains nearly all the performance of the complex model. This hybrid strategy guarantees field deployability while avoiding the fatal performance loss of creating a rule-based score without deep learning guidance.

ideas.title: Distributed High-Throughput Screening via Scalable, Automated Paper-Card Arrays
ideas.description: This redesigned approach maintains the paper-based assay format but re-engineers it for parallel, high-throughput processing without requiring a centralized liquid-handling laboratory. The system uses standardized, multiplexed paper cards arrayed in microplate-compatible cassettes, enabling automated handling by low-cost robotic plate stackers. Detection is achieved via rapid, wide-field imaging with common flatbed scanners or modular smartphone-based readers, allowing multiple sites to run high volumes locally. This hybrid model scales throughput while preserving the low-cost, equipment-light advantages of the original paper platform, enabling decentralized screening networks.

ideas.title: Disposable Smart Cartridge with Integrated, Calibrated Reader Optics
ideas.description: This research develops a credit-card-sized, fully integrated disposable cartridge for paper-based immunoassays that incorporates a micro-optics module—including fixed-focus lenses and calibrated reference elements—within its laminate structure. A separate, ultra-low-cost, portable reader device, containing only a simple CMOS sensor and an LED, interfaces with this cartridge. When inserted, the cartridge's integrated optics automatically align with the reader's sensor, ensuring perfect focus and providing on-board calibration standards for each test, which eliminates the need for user adjustment or periodic reader calibration. This synergy makes the system exceptionally robust for point-of-care use, as the analytical performance is locked into the disposable, guaranteeing result consistency across any number of simple, interchangeable reader units.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Electrochemical Paper Analytical Device (ePAD) Array Platform
ideas.description: This research develops a fully automated, plate-based screening platform where each well contains a miniaturized, electrode-embedded paper analytical device (ePAD). The system leverages robotic liquid handling for high-throughput sample and reagent loading onto the paper-based sensor arrays. Electrochemical detection (e.g., amperometry or voltammetry) provides quantitative, digital readouts with superior sensitivity and a wider dynamic range compared to colorimetric methods. This integrated approach enables rapid, parallel quantitative analysis of multiple targets directly from complex clinical or environmental samples in a centralized laboratory setting.

ideas.title: Dual-Modality, Event-Activated Amplification for Label-Free Vertical Flow Assays
ideas.description: This redesigned vertical flow assay platform uses a two-stage detection mechanism to overcome the sensitivity and specificity limitations of purely label-free approaches. The first stage employs a specific biorecognition element, such as a structure-switching aptamer, immobilized on the paper matrix to capture the target analyte in a label-free manner. The binding event itself triggers the second stage: the activation of a pre-embedded, non-labeled signal amplifier, such as the catalytic release of a dye from a latent substrate or a conductivity change in a polymer layer. This event-activated amplification provides a strong, measurable signal directly correlated with target concentration, achieving high sensitivity without external labels and maintaining robustness against matrix effects.

ideas.title: Apply single-cell RNA-seq barcoding to sample multiplexing

ideas.title: Capacitive Immunosensing on a Paper Flow Platform
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that vertical flow paper assays require gold nanoparticle or other colored/fluorescent labels for detection. By drawing an analogy from semiconductor-based capacitive biosensors, we propose a label-free detection mechanism. The analogous solution uses changes in electrode capacitance upon analyte binding to a functionalized surface. This is adapted by integrating inexpensive, printed interdigitated electrodes into the vertical flow paper matrix. The capture probe is immobilized on the paper in the electrode zone. The binding event alters the dielectric properties of the paper medium between the electrodes, which is detected as a capacitance change. This creates a quantitative, electronic, and entirely label-free assay on a simple paper platform.

ideas.title: Monolithic, Self-Explaining Diagnostic Devices with Embedded XAI
ideas.description: This research develops a monolithic, pre-assembled diagnostic chip that integrates an explainable AI (XAI) model directly into its physical and operational design. The device is engineered so that the primary antigen signal identified by the embedded algorithm triggers a dominant, unambiguous physical change—such as a specific channel filling, a particular region fluorescing, or a designated reservoir depleting—that is visually interpretable without instrumentation. This physically encoded explanation makes the diagnostic result inherently transparent and trustworthy, moving explanation from a digital post-process to a core material property. The synergy lies in using monolithic design principles to physically manifest the XAI output, creating a self-contained device that delivers both a result and an intuitive, built-in rationale for that result.

ideas.title: Modular, Fault-Tolerant Fluidic Cartridge System for Robust Point-of-Care Sample-to-Answer Testing
ideas.description: This redesigned approach decomposes whole blood sample preparation into a series of discrete, self-contained fluidic modules housed within a single-use cartridge. Each module performs a specific unit operation (e.g., plasma separation, cell lysis, nucleic acid binding, wash, elution) and connects to its neighbors via fail-safe interfaces. The host instrument can detect and isolate a malfunctioning module, allowing the test to proceed using a pre-processed sample from a backup cartridge or by rerouting fluid through redundant pathways. This architecture ensures that a single component failure does not compromise the entire test, significantly improving device robustness and reliability in resource-limited settings.

ideas.title: Engineered porous substrates with embedded rule-based diagnostic logic
ideas.description: This research develops low-cost, multiplex diagnostic devices by designing the fluidic pathways and reaction zones within engineered porous polymers or nitrocellulose to execute a pre-defined diagnostic score physically. As the sample flows, specific binding events at controlled zones trigger visible signals whose presence, absence, or sequence directly corresponds to the output of a validated rule-based algorithm, matching the accuracy of a computational model. The device thus performs complex, multi-analyte logic intrinsically through its material design, requiring no external reader, deep learning, or user calculation. This enables robust, deployable diagnostics that are both analytically powerful and operationally simple.

ideas.title: Crosstalk-Immune Multiplexed Sensing via Spatio-Temporal Encoding on a Programmable Membrane
ideas.description: This idea overcomes the fundamental crosstalk limitation of dense spatial multiplexing by integrating active microfluidics with temporal encoding. A programmable fluidic network delivers samples and reagents to the high-density sensor array in precise, timed pulses according to a pre-defined orthogonal codebook. The resulting time-varying signal from the entire membrane is recorded and processed using a computational deconvolution algorithm that separates the contribution of each individually encoded test spot. This approach maintains the high multiplexing density of the original concept while ensuring each analyte measurement is specific, sensitive, and free from interference from neighboring spots.

ideas.title: Active, Sequential Sample Processing Module for Paper-Based Analytical Devices
ideas.description: This redesigned system integrates a hand-powered mechanical actuator with a disposable paper microfluidic cartridge to actively and sequentially process raw biological samples. The user applies force via a plunger or slider, driving the sample through dedicated, in-line stages for filtration, separation, and volumetric dilution under controlled conditions. This active flow control ensures complete and consistent preparation of complex samples like whole blood or viscous fluids, which would otherwise clog passive systems. The result is a robust, sample-agnostic preparation front-end that delivers a standardized analyte-ready fluid to the downstream paper-based detection zone.

ideas.title: Adapting Optical Fiber Bragg Grating Multiplexing for Paper-Based Assays
ideas.description: This idea adapts the wavelength-division multiplexing strategy used in Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensor arrays from the field of fiber optic sensing to increase the multiplex capacity of paper-based lateral flow assays. In FBG systems, multiple gratings on a single fiber reflect unique wavelengths, allowing independent measurement of several parameters. The adapted solution applies this spectral multiplexing concept by functionalizing multiple capture lines on a lateral flow strip with different probes, each paired with a uniquely fluorescent nanocrystal label (e.g., quantum dots). A single sample flow passes all lines, and a compact reader detects the distinct emission spectra from each line, enabling simultaneous, quantitative detection of multiple analytes from one sample application, overcoming traditional spatial multiplexing limits.

ideas.title: Integrated, Disposable Diagnostic Cartridge with Pre-loaded, Sequentially Activated Reagent Packs
ideas.description: This idea adapts the integrated, disposable, and user-friendly design principle from consumer single-use cameras to point-of-care diagnostics. The analogous field is consumer photography, where single-use cameras solved the problem of providing a complete, foolproof photographic system in a sealed, disposable package. The analogous solution involved pre-loading all consumables (film, battery) and designing a simple mechanical interface for user interaction, with post-use processing handled as a single unit. Adapted to the research context, this leads to the development of a fully integrated, single-use, credit-card-sized disposable cartridge. The cartridge contains all necessary liquid reagents pre-stored in a dry or liquid state within fragile, hermetically sealed blisters arranged along a microfluidic circuit. A dedicated, simple reader device would incorporate a mechanical actuator (e.g., a sliding tab or plunger) that, when engaged by the user, physically ruptures these blisters in a pre-programmed sequence. This releases the reagents to passively flow through capillary-driven channels, past detection zones, performing the assay automatically. This design eliminates manual reagent handling, minimizes user error, and ensures complete containment of biohazards within the disposable unit, mirroring the 'shoot, return, and forget' simplicity of a disposable camera.

ideas.title: Integrated Whole-Blood Separation and Reaction on a Patterned Paper Microfluidic Membrane
ideas.description: This research combines the goal of direct whole-blood compatibility with advanced paper patterning to create a multi-functional membrane. Precise hydrophobic barriers, fabricated via wax printing or photolithography, will be designed to incorporate an integrated plasma separation zone, such as a filter or asymmetric channel, that wicks whole blood but only allows plasma to pass into subsequent detection chambers. The assay chemistry in these detection zones can then be optimized specifically for the purified plasma matrix, eliminating interference from blood cells. This results in a simple, one-step, sample-in-answer-out diagnostic device that performs on-board blood fractionation and quantitative analysis.

ideas.title: Smartphone Spectrometer Calibration via Environmental Reference Standards
ideas.description: This idea adapts a calibration solution from environmental remote sensing to the challenge of quantitative color analysis with low-cost smartphone spectrometer attachments. In satellite remote sensing, sensors are routinely calibrated against stable, large-scale environmental references with known spectral signatures to ensure accuracy. The adapted research idea proposes developing a method and companion app where a user first captures a spectrum from a common, pre-characterized reference object present in the measurement environment (such as a standard piece of printer paper or a calibrated color card). The smartphone software then uses the deviation between the measured and the ideal reference spectrum to generate a correction matrix. This matrix is applied in real-time to the spectral data of the target sample, significantly improving measurement consistency and quantitative reliability across different phone models and lighting conditions, enabling robust field science and point-of-care diagnostics.

ideas.title: Adapting Hemodialysis Membrane Principles for Whole Blood Assay Filtration
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from hemodialysis to solve the problem of direct whole blood use in diagnostic assays. In hemodialysis, specially engineered membranes with selective permeability and biocompatible coatings (like heparin) enable continuous, direct contact with blood without clotting or cell lysis. The adapted research idea involves designing a multi-layer assay membrane system. The first layer would act as a size-exclusion filter, mechanically separating plasma from cellular components. The second, functional layer would incorporate surface chemistry mimicking hemodialysis membranes—such as immobilized heparin or other anticoagulant polymers—to prevent platelet activation and clot formation at the membrane interface. This integrated approach would modify both the physical structure and surface chemistry of the assay membrane, enabling robust, one-step application of whole blood by passively filtering plasma into the detection zone while sequestering cells and inhibiting the coagulation cascade directly at the point of sample application.

ideas.title: Lab-on-a-Chip with Microfluidic Sample Processing Inspired by Plant Vascular Systems
ideas.description: This research idea expands the original concept of integrating sample processing by drawing an analogy from biomimetic microfluidics, specifically the study of plant vascular systems. The analogous solution involves using passive, capillary-driven transport and selective filtration—inspired by how xylem and phloem move and filter sap—to create pump-free microfluidic devices. Adapted to the context of a point-of-care blood diagnostic, the idea is to develop a lab-on-a-chip device that employs engineered capillary networks and integrated porous membranes to autonomously wick in a whole blood sample, passively separate plasma from blood cells, and deliver the prepared sample to an on-chip biosensor or reaction zone, thereby creating a true, pump-free, sample-to-answer system.

ideas.title: Adapt Screen-Printed Electrode Fabrication for Paper-Based Electrochemical Lateral Flow Assays
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from the field of Environmental Monitoring and Wearable Sensors, where screen-printed electrodes on flexible substrates are a well-established solution for creating low-cost, disposable electrochemical sensors. The analogous solution involves printing conductive carbon or metal pastes onto porous materials like textiles or membranes to form functional electrode arrays. This solution is adapted to the lateral flow assay context by integrating screen-printing fabrication directly into the lateral flow device manufacturing process. Conductive carbon ink is printed in a precise pattern (e.g., a three-electrode system) onto the nitrocellulose or paper layers before assembly. The result is a fully integrated paper-based electrochemical lateral flow assay that retains the simplicity and rapid flow of a traditional test but replaces subjective colorimetric readout with objective, quantitative electrochemical detection, significantly improving sensitivity and enabling precise measurement of analyte concentration.
